title: Commissioning Design Review Timing
summary: Design review should occur at 100% schematic design, 100% design development, 50% construction documents, and approximately 95% construction documents.
evidence_quote: the commissioning design review could occur four different times during the design: at 100% of schematic design, at 100% of design development, at 50% of complete construction documents, and at approximately 95% of construction documents.

title: Minimum Number of Design Reviews
summary: At least two design reviews are required for commissioning by Standard 189.1.
evidence_quote: having at least two (as required for commissioning by Standard 189.1) provides at least some level of checks and balances.

title: Demand-Based Kitchen Exhaust Systems
summary: Consider demand-based kitchen exhaust systems that vary exhaust airflow based on cooking intensity using smoke and heat sensors.
evidence_quote: Consider the use of demand based kitchen exhaust systems that are exhaust systems that vary the exhaust airflow from the hoods based on the intensity of the cooking based on smoke and heat sensors.

title: Energy Star Commercial Kitchen Appliances Savings
summary: Energy Star commercial kitchen appliances can save between 10% to 65% over conventional appliances depending on the type.
evidence_quote: Energy Star commercial kitchen appliances can save between 10% to 65% over conventional appliances depending on the type.

title: Energy Star Commercial Refrigerators and Freezers Savings
summary: Energy Star qualified commercial refrigerators and freezers can reduce energy costs by as much as 35%.
evidence_quote: Energy Star qualified commercial refrigerators and freezers can reduce energy costs by as much as 35%.

title: Energy Star Commercial Dishwashers Savings
summary: Energy Star qualified commercial dishwashers use approximately 25% less energy and water than standard models.
evidence_quote: Energy Star qualified commercial dishwashers use approximately 25% less energy and water than standard models.

title: Refrigerant Loss Mechanisms
summary: Refrigerant loss occurs primarily through leaks, poor service procedures, contamination, and purge cycles.
evidence_quote: Refrigerant loss occurs primarily through four different channels: leaks, poor service procedures, contamination, and purge cycles.

title: Annual Refrigerant Loss Range
summary: Some chillers lose 2% to 15% of their refrigerant charge annually through different mechanisms.
evidence_quote: Some chillers lose 2% to 15% of their refrigerant charge annually through different mechanisms.

title: High-Pressure Chillers and Purge Units
summary: High-pressure chillers maintain positive pressure throughout the refrigerant circuit and do not require purge units.
evidence_quote: High pressure chillers maintain positive pressure throughout the refrigerant circuit; therefore, they do not require purge units.

title: Sensor Drift Impact on Chiller Staging
summary: Sensors used in chiller staging sequences may drift over time, leading to inaccurate load calculations and disrupted staging.
evidence_quote: These sensors may drift over time, and typical accepted error ranges in these types of sensors will yield inaccurate load calculations that may disrupt proper staging.

title: BAS Performance Requirements Specification
summary: BAS performance requirements should be defined in contract documents per ASHRAE Guideline 13-2015.
evidence_quote: The building automation system (BAS) performance requirements should be defined in the contract documents (i.e., specifications such as those set forth in ASHRAE Guideline 13-2015, Specifying Direct Digital Control Systems [ASHRAE 2014]).

title: Control Sequence Requirements
summary: Control submittals must include a brief overview narrative, interactions with other systems, delineation of localized controls and BAS points, operating modes, capacity control sequences, initial and recommended values for adjustable settings, rough zone analysis, energy mapping, and system override capabilities.
evidence_quote: Ensure that the requirements for the controls submittals in the specifications include statements requiring the following: • A brief overview narrative of the system, generally describing its purpose, components, and function (i.e., the design intent for the controls) • All interactions with other systems • Detailed delineation of interaction between any localized controls and the BAS, listing what points only the BAS monitors and what BAS points are control points and are thus adjustable • Start-up, warm-up, cooldown, occupied, and unoccupied operating modes, plus power failure recovery and alarm sequences • Capacity control sequences and equipment staging • Initial and recommended values for all adjustable settings, setpoints, and parameters that are typically set or adjusted by building operators, as well as any other control settings, fixed values, delays, and so on, that will be useful during testing and ongoing operating the equipment • Rough zone analysis requirements to assure reset strategies are effective • Energy mapping requirements to help operators understand building efficiency • System override capabilities and requirements

title: HRV vs ERV Selection
summary: Generally, an ERV is better for hot and humid climates, and an HRV is better for cold and dry climates, but other factors like dwelling tightness and occupancy are often more important.
evidence_quote: Generally, ERVs are a better choice for hot and humid climates, and HRVs are better for cold and dry climates, but other factors are often more important than the climate, such as dwelling tightness level and the number of occupants.

title: Ventilation Rate Determination
summary: Ventilation rate is typically determined by code, either the International Residential Code (IRC) or ASHRAE Standard 62.2; if no enforceable code, ASHRAE Standard 62.2 is recommended.
evidence_quote: This ventilation rate is typically determined by code, either the International Residential Code (IRC) or ASHRAE Standard 62.2, Ventilation and Indoor Air Quality in Residential Buildings. If there is no enforceable ventilation code in a location, it is recommended that ASHRAE Standard 62.2 be followed.

title: Energy Transfer Principle for HRV/ERV
summary: Energy always transfers from greater to lesser; higher temperature transfers to lower temperature and likewise for moisture differences.
evidence_quote: A general rule to keep in mind is that energy always transfers from greater to lesser. That is, higher temperature transfers to lower temperature and likewise for moisture differences.
